name: Cross-site Scripting (XSS)
description: The product receives untrusted input and incorporates it into a web page that is served to other users without proper escaping. Reflected, stored, and DOM variants exist; all enable execution of attacker-controlled script in the victim's browser. Mitigation: context-aware output encoding, Content-Security-Policy, framework-provided escaping primitives.

name: SQL Injection
description: User-controlled data is concatenated into SQL statements, allowing attackers to alter query semantics, exfiltrate data, or modify rows. Mitigation: parameterized queries / prepared statements; never build SQL via string concatenation.

name: OS Command Injection
description: User-controlled data is passed unescaped to an OS shell or command interpreter, allowing arbitrary command execution. Mitigation: avoid invoking a shell (use exec-family APIs with argv lists); never use shell=True with concatenated input.

name: Path Traversal
description: Untrusted input is used to construct file paths, allowing access to files outside the intended directory using sequences like '..' or absolute paths. Mitigation: canonicalize the path and verify it lives under an allow-listed root.

name: Improper Control of Generation of Code (Code Injection)
description: The product evaluates untrusted input as code, e.g. via eval() / exec() / pickle / Function constructor. Mitigation: eliminate dynamic evaluation; if absolutely required, use a sandboxed parser with an allow-list.

name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
description: Deserializing data from untrusted sources allows attackers to instantiate arbitrary objects, run gadget chains, or trigger RCE. Mitigation: avoid binary deserialization of untrusted data; if unavoidable, sign the payload and use safe loaders (yaml.safe_load, json).
owasp: A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures

name: XML External Entity (XXE)
description: An XML parser resolves external entity references in untrusted XML, enabling SSRF, file disclosure, or DoS. Mitigation: disable DTD processing and external entity resolution in the XML parser.

name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: The server fetches a URL whose target is influenced by user input, allowing attackers to reach internal services or metadata endpoints. Mitigation: allow-list outbound hosts; block link-local / internal ranges; resolve DNS and re-verify after.

name: Use of Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
description: The product uses cryptographic primitives that are broken (MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4) or used in unsafe modes (ECB). Mitigation: prefer AES-GCM, SHA-256+, and password hashes like Argon2id / bcrypt.

name: Use of Insufficiently Random Values
description: Security-sensitive code uses non-cryptographic PRNGs (random.random, Math.random, rand) where unpredictability is required. Mitigation: use secrets / SecureRandom / crypto.randomBytes.

name: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
description: API keys, tokens, or passwords are embedded directly in source code, allowing trivial extraction via git history, decompilation, or memory dumps. Mitigation: load credentials from environment / secret managers; rotate any exposed key.

name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
description: State-changing endpoints accept requests authenticated only via ambient cookies, allowing attackers to issue actions on behalf of victims. Mitigation: SameSite cookies + anti-CSRF tokens + require Origin/Referer checks.

name: Missing Authorization
description: An endpoint or function is accessible without checking that the caller is permitted to perform the action. Mitigation: deny-by-default authorization middleware; centralize policy checks.

name: Incorrect Authorization
description: Authorization logic exists but is incorrect, e.g. comparing user-controlled IDs without role checks (IDOR), or relying on client-sent role claims. Mitigation: centralize and unit-test authorization decisions.

name: Improper Authentication
description: Authentication is missing, weak, or bypassable, e.g. password compared with == (timing leak), no rate limiting, or accepting empty credentials. Mitigation: use vetted auth libraries; constant-time compare; enforce MFA and lockout.

name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
description: Uploads accept arbitrary file types or names, which may be served or executed by the host. Mitigation: validate content-type AND extension AND magic bytes; store outside webroot; serve via a non-executing handler.

name: Out-of-bounds Write
description: Writing past the end of a buffer, typically via strcpy/sprintf/gets or manual pointer arithmetic, leads to memory corruption and RCE. Mitigation: use bounded copy APIs (snprintf, strlcpy); enable stack canaries / ASAN.

name: Out-of-bounds Read
description: Reading past a buffer boundary leaks adjacent memory, e.g. Heartbleed. Mitigation: bound-check indices against length before dereferencing; use slices / spans where available.

name: Use After Free
description: A pointer is dereferenced after the underlying memory has been freed, enabling type confusion, RCE, or info disclosure. Mitigation: null out pointers after free; use RAII / smart pointers; enable ASAN in tests.

name: NULL Pointer Dereference
description: Dereferencing a NULL pointer causes a crash and may be triggerable remotely. Mitigation: check return values from allocators / lookups; treat unchecked nullability as a bug, not a warning.

name: Local File Inclusion (subset of SSRF/Path Traversal)
description: User input controls a path that the server reads or includes, potentially exposing /etc/passwd or executing application-controlled scripts. Mitigation: allow-list templates; never use raw input as a filename.

name: Information Exposure Through Error Messages
description: Stack traces, SQL fragments, or framework debug pages are returned to untrusted users, revealing internal structure that aids further attack. Mitigation: catch-all error handler; log details server-side; show generic message to user.

name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
description: Sensitive data (PII, tokens, secrets) is returned in API responses, logs, or URLs where unauthorized parties can read it. Mitigation: serialize models with explicit allow-listed fields; redact logs.

name: Open Redirect
description: A redirect URL parameter is taken directly from user input, allowing phishing via a trusted host. Mitigation: allow-list internal paths only; refuse cross-origin redirects.

name: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
description: Files / sockets / IPC objects are created with overly broad permissions (e.g. 0o777, world-writable), allowing tampering. Mitigation: principle of least privilege; explicit umask; chmod after creation.
